question: Which term matches this definition: "A named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task, optionally taking inputs and returning a result"?
choices: Class
Function
Variable
Library

explanation.summary: A function is one of the most basic building blocks in any programming language. It's a named chunk of instructions you can run whenever you need that specific task done, instead of retyping the same steps over and over.
lineByLine: "Named" — you give it a label, like `calculateTotal` or `fetchWeather`, so you can refer to it later.
"Reusable" — once written, you can call it as many times as you want from different places.
"Optionally taking inputs" — a function can accept values (called parameters) to work with, like a recipe taking ingredients.
"Returning a result" — many functions hand back a value when they're done, like a vending machine giving you a snack after you press a button.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a function is like a recipe card. It has a name ("Make Pancakes"), it might take ingredients (inputs), and it produces a result (pancakes). In an Android app, `fun calculateAverage(temps: List<Int>): Int` is a function — you give it a list of temperatures, and it hands back one average number.

question: What is a "Function"?
choices: A blueprint used to create objects
A named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task, optionally taking inputs and returning a result
A file that lists everything an app is allowed to do
The final installable file for an Android app

explanation.summary: A function is a named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task. It can take inputs (called parameters) and can return a result when it's done.
lineByLine: Functions let you write a piece of logic ONE time and reuse it anywhere it's needed, instead of copy-pasting the same code repeatedly.
A function's name should describe what it does, like `sendEmail` or `formatDate`.
Some functions take inputs (parameters) to customize what they do, and some return a result back to whoever called them.
Functions are the basic unit of "doing something" in almost every programming language, including Kotlin.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: think of a function like a recipe card — it has a name, it might need ingredients (inputs), and it produces something when followed (a result). In an app, a function like `fun login(username: String, password: String): Boolean` takes two inputs and returns true or false depending on whether the login worked.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A blueprint that defines the properties and behaviors of a type of object; you create actual objects from it"?
choices: Class
Function
Package
Resource

explanation.summary: A class is a blueprint — it describes what a type of thing should look like and what it can do, but it isn't a real, usable thing by itself. You use a class to create actual objects, each with their own data.
lineByLine: "Blueprint" — a class describes the shape of something, like what properties (data) and functions (behavior) it has.
"Properties" — the pieces of data a class defines, like a `User` class having a `name` and an `age`.
"Behaviors" — the functions a class defines, like a `User` class having a `sendMessage()` function.
"You create actual objects from it" — the class itself is just the plan; each real, usable version made from that plan is called an object.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a class is like a blueprint for a house. The blueprint itself isn't a house you can live in — it's just the plan. You can build many actual houses (objects) from the same blueprint (class), and each house can have its own paint color (its own data), even though they all follow the same basic design.

question: What is a "Class"?
choices: A special value meaning no value at all
A blueprint that defines the properties and behaviors of a type of object; you create actual objects from it
A named container that holds a value which can change
A defined set of tools that lets two pieces of software talk to each other

explanation.summary: A class is a blueprint describing what a type of thing should look like (its properties) and what it can do (its behaviors/functions). It's not a real, usable thing on its own — you use the class to create actual objects.
lineByLine: Classes group related data and functions together into one named blueprint, like `Dog` or `WeatherForecast`.
Properties are the pieces of data the class holds, like a `Dog` class having a `name` and a `breed`.
Functions defined inside a class describe its behaviors, like a `Dog` class having a `bark()` function.
You never use the class directly — you create individual objects from it, each with their own specific data.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a class is like a cookie cutter. The cutter itself isn't a cookie — it's the shape used to make many actual cookies (objects) from dough. In Kotlin, `class User(val name: String, val age: Int)` is a blueprint; `User("Alice", 30)` creates one actual object following that blueprint.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A specific instance created from a class, holding its own data based on that class's blueprint"?
choices: Class
Object
Variable
Function

explanation.summary: An object is a real, usable thing created from a class's blueprint. While the class describes what's possible, the object is an actual, specific version with its own real data.
lineByLine: "Specific instance" — one actual, real example created from a class, as opposed to the abstract blueprint itself.
"Created from a class" — you always need a class first; the object is built following that class's design.
"Holding its own data" — even though many objects can come from the same class, each one can have different actual values.
This is why you might create many objects from one class — like many `User` objects, each with a different name and age.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: if a class is a cookie cutter, an object is one actual cookie made using it. Every cookie shares the same shape (the class's design), but each one is a separate, real cookie you can actually eat. In code, `val myDog = Dog("Rex", "Labrador")` creates one object — a real, specific dog — following the blueprint of the `Dog` class.

question: What is an "Object"?
choices: A file describing an app's structure and rules
A specific instance created from a class, holding its own data based on that class's blueprint
A collection of pre-written code you can reuse
The environment where compiled code actually runs on a device

explanation.summary: An object is a real, specific instance created from a class. The class is the blueprint; the object is an actual usable thing built following that blueprint, with its own real values filled in.
lineByLine: Every object comes from exactly one class — the class defines what properties and functions the object will have.
Multiple objects can be created from the same class, each with its own independent data.
Changing one object's data doesn't affect any other object created from the same class.
"Creating an object" is often called instantiating a class — making one specific instance of it.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: if a class is a cookie cutter, an object is one actual cookie. You can make many cookies (objects) from one cutter (class), and each cookie is separate — frosting one doesn't frost the others. In an app, `val forecast = Weather(city = "Seattle", temp = 62)` creates one real object holding specific data, following the `Weather` class's blueprint.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A named container that holds a value, which can be read or (if mutable) changed later"?
choices: Variable
Object
Null
Function

explanation.summary: A variable is a named spot in the program's memory that holds a value. You give it a name so you can refer to that value later, and depending on how it's declared, that value might be allowed to change over time.
lineByLine: "Named container" — a variable has a label (like `age` or `userName`) so the program (and you) can refer to it.
"Holds a value" — the variable stores something, like a number, a piece of text, or an object.
"Can be read" — you can look at what value the variable currently holds.
"If mutable, changed later" — some variables can be reassigned to a new value; others are locked once set.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a variable is like a labeled jar on a shelf. The label is the variable's name, and whatever is inside the jar is its current value. Some jars (like Kotlin's `val`) are sealed once filled — you can look inside, but never swap the contents. Other jars (like Kotlin's `var`) can be emptied and refilled with something new later.

question: What is a "Variable"?
choices: A named container that holds a value, which can be read or (if mutable) changed later
A special value meaning no value at all
The final installable file for an Android app
A tool for finding and fixing problems in running code

explanation.summary: A variable is a named container for a value. Its name lets your code refer back to that value later, and depending on how the variable is declared, it may or may not be allowed to change after it's first set.
lineByLine: Every variable has a name, chosen by the programmer, that describes what it's holding.
The value inside a variable could be many kinds of things: a number, a piece of text, true/false, or even a whole object.
In Kotlin, a `val` variable is fixed once assigned — you can't reassign it. A `var` variable CAN be reassigned to a different value later.
Variables are how a program keeps track of information as it runs.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a variable is like a labeled jar. "score" might be the label, and the number inside is the current value. A sealed jar (val) can be looked at but never refilled. An open jar (var) can be emptied and refilled anytime. In an app, `var tapCount = 0` is a variable that starts at 0 and can be updated as the user taps a button.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A special value meaning 'no value at all' — the absence of any real data"?
choices: Variable
Object
Null
Class

explanation.summary: Null represents the complete absence of a value. It's not zero, it's not an empty piece of text — it means there's genuinely nothing there at all, which is an important, common situation to plan for in code.
lineByLine: Null is different from a value like 0 or an empty string — those are still real, existing values. Null means there's no value whatsoever.
A variable can sometimes be null if it's declared as "nullable" — meaning the programmer expects it might not have a real value yet.
Trying to use a null value as if it were real (like calling a function on it) can cause errors, so code often has to check for null first.
Handling null carefully is a big part of writing reliable code, especially in Kotlin, which has strong built-in tools for this.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: null is like an empty mailbox with no letter inside — not a letter that says "nothing," but a complete absence of any letter at all. In an app, `val city: String? = null` means the city variable is allowed to have no value yet, which the app must check for before trying to use it.

question: What does "Null" mean?
choices: A blueprint used to create objects
A named container that holds a value
A special value meaning 'no value at all' — the absence of any real data
A collection of pre-written code you can reuse

explanation.summary: Null means the complete absence of a value — not zero, not an empty piece of text, but genuinely nothing at all. It's a normal, expected situation in code that has to be planned for carefully.
lineByLine: A variable holding null isn't holding "an empty version" of something — it's holding nothing whatsoever.
Some variables are allowed to be null (called "nullable"), and some are never allowed to be null.
Code that assumes a value is never null, when it actually can be, is one of the most common sources of crashes in software.
Kotlin has special tools (like `?.` and `?:`) specifically designed to safely handle values that might be null.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: null is like an empty parking spot — there's a spot (the variable exists), but there's no car in it (no actual value). In an app, if a user hasn't set a profile picture yet, `val profilePhoto: String? = null` correctly represents that there simply isn't one yet, rather than pretending there's a blank picture.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A defined set of tools and rules that lets one piece of software talk to another, without needing to know its internal details"?
choices: Library
API
Framework
SDK

explanation.summary: An API (Application Programming Interface) is like an agreed-upon menu of requests one piece of software can make to another. You don't need to know how the other side works internally — you just need to know what you can ask for and what you'll get back.
lineByLine: "Defined set of tools and rules" — an API specifies exactly what requests are allowed and what format they need to be in.
"Lets one piece of software talk to another" — this could be your app talking to a weather service, or one part of your own code talking to another part.
"Without needing to know its internal details" — you don't need to understand HOW the weather service calculates temperature, just how to ask for it and read the answer.
APIs exist at many levels: a weather company's API, Android's own system APIs, or even a simple API made of a few functions inside your own app.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: an API is like a restaurant menu. You don't need to know how the kitchen works — you just need to know what you can order (the menu items) and what you'll get back (the dish). In an app, calling a weather API means sending a request like "give me today's forecast for Seattle" and getting structured data back, without knowing how that service gathers its weather data.

question: What is an "API"?
choices: A pre-written collection of code you can add to your project
A defined set of tools and rules that lets one piece of software talk to another, without needing to know its internal details
The final installable file for an Android app
A tool for finding and fixing problems in running code

explanation.summary: An API (Application Programming Interface) is an agreed-upon set of requests one piece of software can make to another, along with the rules for how to ask and what you'll get back — all without needing to understand the other side's internal workings.
lineByLine: APIs define exactly which requests are allowed, what information they need, and what format the response will be in.
The word "interface" is key — it's the boundary between two systems, hiding the complicated internal details on either side.
Apps commonly use APIs to fetch data from the internet, like weather, news, or maps information.
Android itself provides many APIs your app can use, like ones for accessing the camera, location, or notifications.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: an API is like a restaurant menu — you order by name, without knowing the kitchen's recipes. In an app, `WeatherApi.fetchLatestForecasts()` is a call to an API — you ask for the latest forecasts, and you get structured data back, without knowing how that weather service actually calculates its predictions.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A pre-written collection of code you can add to your project to reuse functionality, without writing it yourself"?
choices: Framework
API
Library
Module

explanation.summary: A library is a bundle of already-written code that solves a specific problem, which you can add to your own project and use directly, saving you from writing that same functionality from scratch.
lineByLine: "Pre-written collection of code" — someone else already solved a problem and packaged the solution up for others to use.
"Add to your project" — you pull the library in (often as a dependency, declared in Gradle) rather than copy-pasting its code.
"Reuse functionality" — you call the library's functions to get useful behavior, like formatting dates or loading images.
"Without writing it yourself" — this saves enormous amounts of time compared to solving every problem from scratch.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a library is like buying a pre-made cake mix instead of inventing your own recipe from raw ingredients. You still bake the cake (use the library in your code), but someone else already figured out the tricky parts. In Android, Room (for databases) and Retrofit (for network calls) are popular libraries apps commonly add as dependencies.

question: What is a "Library"?
choices: A larger structure that provides the overall shape of an app and calls your code at the right moments
A pre-written collection of code you can add to your project to reuse functionality, without writing it yourself
A specific instance created from a class
The environment where compiled code actually runs on a device

explanation.summary: A library is a pre-written bundle of code that solves a specific problem, ready for you to add to your own project and use, instead of writing that same functionality yourself.
lineByLine: Libraries are typically added to a project as a dependency, declared in a file like build.gradle.kts.
You call functions from the library the same way you'd call functions you wrote yourself.
A library usually focuses on one specific job — like handling network requests, or working with dates and times.
Using well-tested libraries instead of writing everything from scratch saves time and reduces bugs.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a library is like a pre-made cake mix — you still do the baking, but someone else figured out the tricky recipe details. In an app's build.gradle.kts, a line like `implementation("androidx.room:room-runtime:2.6.1")` adds the Room library, giving your project ready-made tools for working with a database.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A larger structure that provides the overall shape of an app and calls YOUR code at the right moments, rather than the other way around"?
choices: Library
Framework
API
Module

explanation.summary: A framework is bigger than a library — instead of you calling its functions when you need them, a framework provides the overall structure of your app and decides WHEN to call the specific pieces of code you've written to fit inside that structure.
lineByLine: With a library, YOU are in control — you call the library's functions whenever you decide you need them.
With a framework, the framework is in control — it calls YOUR code at specific moments it decides are appropriate.
Android itself is a framework: it decides when to call your Activity's onCreate, when to call your BroadcastReceiver's onReceive, and so on.
This difference (who's in charge) is sometimes called "inversion of control" — you're not driving; the framework is, and it invites your code along for specific parts of the ride.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a library is like a toolbox you reach into whenever you need a specific tool. A framework is more like a factory assembly line — it has its own overall process, and it calls on you (your code) to do specific jobs at specific points along the line. Android itself is a framework: it decides when your Activity's `onCreate` runs, not the other way around.

question: What is a "Framework"?
choices: A pre-written collection of code you call whenever you need it
A larger structure that provides the overall shape of an app and calls YOUR code at the right moments, rather than the other way around
A named container that holds a value
A file describing an app's structure and rules

explanation.summary: A framework provides the overall shape and structure of an app, and it's the one that decides when to call specific pieces of code you've written — flipping the usual relationship where you're the one calling functions when you need them.
lineByLine: Unlike a library (which you call), a framework calls YOU — it invokes your code at moments it has decided are appropriate.
Android is a framework: it decides when to run your Activity's onCreate, when your BroadcastReceiver's onReceive fires, and so on.
You write code that fits INTO the framework's structure, following its rules about naming, structure, and behavior.
This flipped relationship (the framework is in charge, not you) is a defining feature that separates a framework from a plain library.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a library is a toolbox you reach into when you need something. A framework is more like a factory assembly line that calls on you to do specific jobs at specific points. Android itself is exactly this kind of framework — you never directly call your own `onCreate()`; the Android OS decides when to call it for you.

question: Which term matches this definition: "The environment where your compiled code actually executes on a real device, as opposed to when it's being written or built"?
choices: IDE
SDK
Runtime
Framework

explanation.summary: The runtime is the phase where your app is actually running and doing things on a real device (or emulator), as opposed to the earlier phases of writing the code or building/compiling it into a finished app.
lineByLine: Writing code happens in an editor, before anything runs — this is not the runtime.
Building/compiling turns your written code into something a device can actually run — still not the runtime itself.
Runtime is the actual moment the app is executing — buttons are being tapped, screens are appearing, data is being fetched.
Some errors only show up "at runtime" — meaning the code looked fine while writing/building it, but caused a problem once it was actually running.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: writing a recipe is like writing code. Baking it in the oven is the build process. Actually eating and tasting the cake is the runtime — the moment where you experience whether it truly works. In an app, a crash that only happens when a specific button is tapped is a "runtime" problem — it wasn't caught while writing or building the code.

question: What does "Runtime" refer to?
choices: A dedicated program for writing, building, and testing code
The environment where your compiled code actually executes on a real device, as opposed to when it's being written or built
A collection of libraries, tools, and APIs for a specific platform
A blueprint that defines the properties and behaviors of a type of object

explanation.summary: Runtime describes the actual period when an app is executing and doing things on a device — as opposed to the earlier stages of writing the code or compiling/building it into a finished, runnable form.
lineByLine: Code goes through several stages: being written, being built/compiled, and finally running (the runtime).
Some problems (called runtime errors) only appear once the app is actually running — the code looked fine when written and compiled, but breaks in an actual real situation.
The Manifest lesson from earlier — an undeclared Activity crashing when launched — is a classic runtime problem, since it compiles just fine but fails once actually running.
"Runtime" is also sometimes used to describe the underlying system that manages your code while it executes.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: writing a recipe is writing code. Baking it is building/compiling. Actually eating it and finding out if it tastes good is the runtime — the real, live experience of it working (or not). In an app, launching an undeclared Activity compiles just fine but crashes at runtime, once the app actually tries to do it.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A dedicated program for writing, building, and testing code, combining an editor, compiler, and other tools into one workspace"?
choices: SDK
IDE
API
Library

explanation.summary: An IDE (Integrated Development Environment) is the main program developers use to actually write and work with their code. Android Studio is the specific IDE built for Android app development.
lineByLine: "Integrated" — many separate tools (editor, compiler, debugger, emulator control) are combined into one single program.
"Writing" — the IDE provides a smart text editor for writing code, often catching mistakes as you type.
"Building" — the IDE can trigger the build process (using Gradle) to turn your code into a runnable app.
"Testing" — the IDE lets you run your app on an emulator or real device, and inspect what's happening while it runs.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: an IDE is like a fully-equipped workshop, rather than just a single tool. Instead of a bare notepad for writing and a separate program for building, an IDE like Android Studio puts the editor, the build tools, and the testing tools all in one place. Every Android developer spends most of their time working directly inside Android Studio.

question: What is an "IDE" (like Android Studio)?
choices: A collection of libraries, tools, and APIs for a specific platform
A dedicated program for writing, building, and testing code, combining an editor, compiler, and other tools into one workspace
The final installable file for an Android app
A stream of values over time that code can continuously observe

explanation.summary: An IDE (Integrated Development Environment) is the main application developers use day-to-day to write code, build it into a runnable app, and test it — combining many separate tools into one unified workspace. Android Studio is the official IDE for Android development.
lineByLine: The IDE's text editor helps you write code, often highlighting mistakes or suggesting corrections as you type.
The IDE can trigger Gradle to build your project into an actual runnable app.
The IDE lets you launch your app on an emulator or a real connected device to see it actually running.
Tools like Logcat (for viewing debug messages) are typically built directly into the IDE as well.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: an IDE is like a fully-equipped workshop rather than a single bare tool — everything you need is in one place. Android Studio specifically is the IDE built for Android development, combining code editing, Gradle builds, and device testing into one program.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A collection of libraries, tools, and APIs specifically for building apps for a particular platform, like Android"?
choices: IDE
SDK
Gradle
Library

explanation.summary: An SDK (Software Development Kit) is a bundled package of everything you need to build apps for a specific platform — including libraries, APIs, and tools tailored to that platform's requirements.
lineByLine: "Collection of libraries, tools, and APIs" — the SDK isn't just one thing, it's a whole toolkit bundled together.
"Specifically for building apps for a particular platform" — the Android SDK is built specifically for making Android apps, with tools and APIs that only make sense on Android.
The Android SDK includes things like the APIs for Activities, Services, and system features, plus tools to build and package your app.
You typically install and manage the SDK through your IDE (like Android Studio), which handles keeping the right pieces available.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: if a library is one tool and a framework is the assembly line, an SDK is the entire equipped workshop plus the manual for building one specific kind of thing — in this case, Android apps. When you set up a new Android project, Android Studio automatically manages which pieces of the Android SDK your project needs.

question: What is an "SDK"?
choices: A collection of libraries, tools, and APIs specifically for building apps for a particular platform, like Android
A dedicated program for writing, building, and testing code
A tool for finding and fixing problems in running code
The final installable file for an Android app

explanation.summary: An SDK (Software Development Kit) bundles together everything needed to build apps for a specific platform — APIs, libraries, and supporting tools — all tailored to that one platform's specific needs.
lineByLine: "compileSdk", "minSdk", and "targetSdk" (seen in a project's build.gradle.kts) all refer to specific versions of the Android SDK.
The SDK provides the actual APIs your code calls to do Android-specific things, like starting an Activity or accessing a Service.
Different SDK versions correspond to different Android OS versions, each potentially adding new capabilities.
Android Studio manages downloading and updating the SDK components your projects need.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: an SDK is like a specialized construction kit for building one particular kind of structure — it has exactly the right tools and materials for that job, and nothing else. The Android SDK gives developers everything specifically needed to build Android apps, as opposed to a general-purpose programming toolkit.

question: Which term matches this definition: "The process (and tool) for finding and fixing problems in code by inspecting messages and errors the app produces while running"?
choices: Runtime
Debugging / Logcat
Gradle
API

explanation.summary: Debugging is the overall process of figuring out why something isn't working correctly. Logcat is Android Studio's specific tool for viewing the stream of messages, warnings, and errors an app produces while it's actually running.
lineByLine: "Finding and fixing problems" — debugging is the whole activity of tracking down why something is broken and correcting it.
"Inspecting messages and errors" — Logcat shows a running feed of text output from the app, including your own debug messages and system-level errors.
Developers often deliberately add print statements or log calls (`Log.d(...)`) specifically to help debug a tricky problem.
When an app crashes, Logcat usually shows a detailed "stack trace" — a trail of information about exactly where and why the crash happened.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: debugging is like being a detective, and Logcat is your notebook of clues. If a car makes a weird noise, a mechanic listens carefully and checks gauges to figure out what's wrong — that's debugging. Logcat is the running log where Android shows you exactly what your app is doing (and where it's going wrong) as it happens.

question: What does "Debugging" (using a tool like Logcat) mean?
choices: The process (and tool) for finding and fixing problems in code by inspecting messages and errors the app produces while running
The final installable file for an Android app
A named container that holds a value
A larger structure that calls your code at the right moments

explanation.summary: Debugging is the process of finding and fixing problems in code. Logcat is the specific tool in Android Studio that shows a running feed of messages, warnings, and errors your app produces while it's actually executing, which is essential for figuring out what's actually going wrong.
lineByLine: When an app doesn't behave as expected, debugging is the process of investigating why.
Logcat displays a continuous stream of text, including messages your own code deliberately logs, and errors the system reports automatically.
A crash typically produces a detailed error trail in Logcat, showing exactly which line of code caused the problem.
Skilled debugging often means reading Logcat carefully, forming a guess about the cause, and testing that guess.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: debugging is being a detective, and Logcat is your notebook of clues from the scene. If a car makes a strange noise, a mechanic debugs the issue by listening and checking gauges. In an app, if a button doesn't do what it should, checking Logcat for error messages is usually the very first step toward figuring out why.

question: Which term matches this definition: "The build system that assembles all of a project's code, resources, and dependencies into a finished, installable app"?
choices: Gradle
SDK
IDE
API

explanation.summary: Gradle is the tool responsible for taking everything in your project — your Kotlin code, your resources (like images and text), and any libraries you're using — and combining them all into one finished, runnable app.
lineByLine: Gradle reads configuration files (build.gradle.kts) that describe what your project needs: which SDK version, which dependencies, and other settings.
It compiles your Kotlin code into a form the device can actually run.
It bundles in your resources — images, text, layouts — alongside the compiled code.
The end result of a Gradle build is an installable app file, ready to run on a device.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: Gradle is like a factory assembly line for your app. It takes raw materials (your code, images, and libraries) and runs them through a series of steps to produce a finished product (a runnable app) at the end. Every time you click "Run" in Android Studio, Gradle is working behind the scenes to assemble everything.

question: What is "Gradle"?
choices: A dedicated program for writing, building, and testing code
The build system that assembles all of a project's code, resources, and dependencies into a finished, installable app
A single path of execution that can run code
The set of states a component moves through over time

explanation.summary: Gradle is the build system responsible for combining everything in an Android project — code, resources, and any external libraries (dependencies) — into one finished, installable app.
lineByLine: Gradle reads project configuration from files ending in .gradle.kts, describing exactly what the build needs.
It handles compiling code, bundling resources, and pulling in any declared dependencies (libraries).
Different modules in a larger project each typically have their own Gradle configuration.
The output of a successful Gradle build is a runnable app file, ready to install on a device.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: Gradle is like a factory assembly line — raw materials (your code, images, and libraries) go in one end, and a finished, packaged product (a runnable app) comes out the other. Whenever you press "Run" in Android Studio, Gradle is doing this assembly work behind the scenes.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A self-contained section of a project (like 'app') that Gradle builds somewhat independently, often with its own dependencies"?
choices: Package
Module
Dependency
Resource

explanation.summary: A module is one distinct, self-contained part of a larger project. The most common module in a simple Android project is called "app" — it holds the actual application code. Bigger projects can have multiple modules working together.
lineByLine: "Self-contained section" — a module has its own folder, its own build.gradle.kts, and its own specific purpose.
"Gradle builds somewhat independently" — each module can be built and configured somewhat separately, even though they combine into one final app.
"Often with its own dependencies" — one module might need a networking library, while another module doesn't.
A project's settings.gradle.kts file lists every module that's part of the overall build.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: think of a module like one department in a company — it has its own team and its own specific job, but it still works together with other departments to produce the company's overall product. A simple Android project usually has just one module ("app"), but larger projects might split things into several modules, like "app", "core-network", and "feature-login".

question: What is a "Module" in an Android project?
choices: A special value meaning no value at all
A self-contained section of a project (like 'app') that Gradle builds somewhat independently, often with its own dependencies
A message the operating system displays outside the app itself
A stream of values over time that code can continuously observe

explanation.summary: A module is a self-contained section of a project, with its own folder and build configuration, that Gradle can build somewhat independently. The most common module in a simple project is "app," but larger projects can have several modules working together.
lineByLine: Every module has its own build.gradle.kts file, describing its specific dependencies and settings.
A project's settings.gradle.kts registers which modules are actually included in the build.
Splitting a project into multiple modules (like a shared "core" module and several "feature" modules) is common in larger, professional apps.
Even with multiple modules, they all ultimately combine into one final app when built.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a module is like one department in a company — it has its own specific job and team, but still works together with the rest of the company toward one final product. A simple app might have just the "app" module; a larger app might split work across "app", "core-network", and "feature-login" modules.

question: Which term matches this definition: "An external library or module that your project relies on and pulls in, rather than writing that functionality yourself"?
choices: Module
Dependency
Package
Resource

explanation.summary: A dependency is something your project needs from outside itself — usually a library — that it pulls in and relies on, instead of the developer writing that same functionality from scratch.
lineByLine: "External library or module" — a dependency is code that comes from somewhere other than your own hand-written app code.
"Your project relies on" — your code calls functions from that dependency, expecting it to be available.
"Pulls in" — Gradle downloads and includes the dependency automatically, based on what's declared in build.gradle.kts.
Common examples of dependencies include Room (for databases) or Retrofit (for network calls) — both are libraries most apps don't write from scratch.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a dependency is like ordering a part from a supplier instead of manufacturing it yourself. If you're building a car, you probably don't forge your own bolts — you rely on (depend on) a supplier for standard parts. In an app's build.gradle.kts, a line like `implementation("androidx.room:room-runtime:2.6.1")` declares a dependency on the Room library.

question: What is a "Dependency" in an Android project?
choices: An external library or module that your project relies on and pulls in, rather than writing that functionality yourself
A message the operating system displays outside the app itself
A single path of execution that can run code
A tool that decides which thread a coroutine's work should run on

explanation.summary: A dependency is an external library (or another module) that your project relies on and pulls in through Gradle, instead of the developer writing that same functionality from scratch themselves.
lineByLine: Dependencies are declared in a module's build.gradle.kts file, usually with an `implementation(...)` line.
Gradle automatically downloads and includes each declared dependency when building the project.
Your own code can then call functions from that dependency as if it were part of your own project.
Relying on well-tested dependencies (like Room or Retrofit) saves significant time compared to building the same functionality yourself.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a dependency is like ordering a part from a supplier instead of making it yourself — if you're building furniture, you probably buy the screws rather than forging your own. In Android, declaring `implementation("androidx.room:room-runtime:2.6.1")` in build.gradle.kts tells Gradle "this project depends on the Room library — please include it."

question: Which term matches this definition: "The final, installable package file that contains a compiled Android app, ready to run on a device"?
choices: Module
Dependency
APK
SDK

explanation.summary: An APK (Android Package) is the actual file format used to distribute and install a finished Android app. It's the end result of Gradle's build process — everything your app needs, packaged into one file.
lineByLine: "Final, installable package file" — an APK is a single file that a device can install, unlike your raw project source code.
"Contains a compiled Android app" — inside an APK is your compiled code, your resources (images, text), and the Manifest, all bundled together.
"Ready to run on a device" — once installed, the OS knows how to launch and run the app described by that APK.
When you download an app from the Play Store, you're effectively downloading (a more modern variant of) an APK.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: an APK is like a sealed, ready-to-ship box containing a fully assembled product. Everything needed is already inside — you don't need to build anything yourself, you just install it. When Gradle finishes building a project, the output is an APK (or a related format called an App Bundle) ready to install on a phone.

question: What is an "APK"?
choices: A collection of libraries, tools, and APIs for a specific platform
The final, installable package file that contains a compiled Android app, ready to run on a device
A file that officially declares every component, permission, and rule an app needs
The build system that assembles a project into a finished app

explanation.summary: An APK (Android Package) is the actual installable file format for a finished Android app — the output of the Gradle build process, containing compiled code, resources, and the Manifest all bundled together.
lineByLine: An APK is what actually gets installed on a device — you can't directly install raw project source code.
Building a project (via Gradle) is the process that produces an APK from your code and resources.
Once installed from an APK, the device's operating system reads the app's Manifest to know how to run it.
A related, more modern format called an App Bundle (AAB) is what's typically uploaded to the Play Store today, but it serves a similar purpose.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: an APK is like a fully sealed, ready-to-ship box containing an assembled product — nothing more needs to be built, you just install it. Every Android app you've ever installed arrived on your device in essentially this kind of package.

question: Which term matches this definition: "The file that officially declares every component, permission, and rule an Android app needs — nothing runs without being listed here"?
choices: Manifest
Package
Resource
Module

explanation.summary: The Manifest (AndroidManifest.xml) is the single file where an app officially declares everything the operating system needs to know about it: every screen, every background helper, every sensitive permission it might request.
lineByLine: "Officially declares" — writing something in code isn't enough; it must also appear in the Manifest to be recognized by the OS.
"Every component" — Activities, Services, and BroadcastReceivers all need their own entry here.
"Permission" — sensitive capabilities (like using the camera) are also declared in the Manifest.
"Nothing runs without being listed here" — even a perfectly coded screen will crash if launched without a matching Manifest entry.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: the Manifest is like a school's front-office binder listing every classroom, teacher, and rule officially recognized by the school. A classroom that's built but never added to the binder doesn't officially exist as far as the school is concerned. In an app, forgetting to add a new screen to the Manifest is one of the most common beginner mistakes — the code compiles fine, but crashes the moment it's actually launched.

question: What is the "Manifest" (AndroidManifest.xml)?
choices: A text-based format using nested tags to describe structured information
The file that officially declares every component, permission, and rule an Android app needs — nothing runs without being listed here
A named container that holds a value
The tool that assembles a project's code and files into an app

explanation.summary: The Manifest is a required file, written in XML, that officially declares everything the Android operating system needs to know about an app — every screen, every background helper, and every sensitive permission it might request.
lineByLine: Every Activity, Service, and BroadcastReceiver must have a matching entry in the Manifest to be recognized by the OS.
Permissions the app needs (like using the camera) are also declared in the Manifest.
The Manifest also declares app-wide settings, like the app's icon and display name.
A component that exists in code but is missing from the Manifest will compile fine but crash the moment the app tries to actually use it.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: the Manifest is like a school's front-office binder listing every classroom and rule the school officially recognizes. A room that's built but not written in the binder doesn't officially exist to the school. Similarly, an app screen (Activity) not declared in the Manifest doesn't officially exist to the Android OS, even if the code is otherwise perfect.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A naming convention (like a folder path) that uniquely identifies where a piece of code belongs, often matching an app's unique ID"?
choices: Module
Package
Resource
Dependency

explanation.summary: A package is a naming system (similar to a folder structure) used to organize and uniquely identify code. In Android, the package name is often the same as the app's unique application ID, distinguishing it from every other app.
lineByLine: "Naming convention (like a folder path)" — package names use dots to separate parts, like `com.example.weathernow`.
"Uniquely identifies where a piece of code belongs" — a Kotlin file's package declaration matches the actual folder structure it lives in.
"Often matching an app's unique ID" — the applicationId in Gradle is frequently the same as the app's main package name.
This naming system prevents naming collisions — two different developers' `MainActivity` classes won't conflict if they're in different packages.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a package name is like a full mailing address, ensuring your letter reaches the right house even if many other houses share the same street name elsewhere. A class named `MainActivity` in `com.example.weathernow` is a completely different thing from a class also named `MainActivity` in `com.example.somethingelse` — the package makes each one uniquely identifiable.

question: What is a "Package" in Android/Kotlin?
choices: A stream of values over time that code can continuously observe
A naming convention (like a folder path) that uniquely identifies where a piece of code belongs, often matching an app's unique ID
The set of states a component moves through over time
A message the operating system displays outside the app itself

explanation.summary: A package is a dot-separated naming system used to organize code and uniquely identify where it belongs, similar to a folder path. It's commonly the same as an app's unique application ID.
lineByLine: A package name looks like `com.example.weathernow`, with each dot-separated part corresponding to an actual folder in the project.
Every Kotlin file starts with a package declaration matching its actual folder location.
This system prevents naming collisions between different developers' code that might otherwise use the same class names.
In the Manifest, `android:name=".MainActivity"` uses a leading dot as shorthand for "this app's package plus MainActivity."
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a package name is like a full mailing address — it ensures a letter reaches exactly the right house, even if other houses elsewhere share the same street name. Two developers can each have a class called `MainActivity`, but as long as they're in different packages (like `com.example.appone` vs `com.example.apptwo`), there's no conflict.

question: Which term matches this definition: "Non-code assets a project uses, like images, text, and colors, kept separate from code so they're easy to change or swap"?
choices: Dependency
Resource
Module
Package

explanation.summary: A resource is any non-code content an app needs — images, text strings, colors, and more — kept in dedicated files separate from the actual programming logic, so they can be updated easily without touching code.
lineByLine: "Non-code assets" — resources are things like pictures, text, and colors, not programming logic.
"Images, text, and colors" — these live in the res/ folder, organized into type-specific subfolders like drawable/, values/, and layout/.
"Kept separate from code" — this separation means a translator can update text, or a designer can update a color, without touching any Kotlin code at all.
Code refers to resources indirectly (through the R class), so changing a resource's value updates it everywhere it's used, automatically.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a resource is like the ingredients list on a recipe card, kept separate from the cooking instructions. You can swap in a different brand of flour (a resource change) without rewriting the whole recipe (the code). In Android, updating the text in strings.xml (a resource file) updates that text everywhere the app displays it, with zero code changes required.

question: What is a "Resource" in an Android project?
choices: A named container that holds a value
Non-code assets a project uses, like images, text, and colors, kept separate from code so they're easy to change or swap
A tool that decides which thread a coroutine's work should run on
A defined set of tools that lets two pieces of software talk to each other

explanation.summary: A resource is any non-code content an app needs, like images, text, and colors, stored in dedicated files (usually inside the res/ folder) separate from the app's actual programming logic.
lineByLine: Resources are organized by type into subfolders: drawable/ for images, values/ for text and colors, layout/ for screen layouts.
Kotlin code refers to resources indirectly through the auto-generated R class, like `R.string.app_name`.
Because code only holds a reference (not a direct copy), updating a resource's value updates it everywhere automatically.
This separation makes it easy for translators or designers to update text and colors without ever touching Kotlin code.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a resource is like the ingredients list on a recipe card, kept separate from the actual cooking steps. You can swap in a different ingredient without rewriting the whole recipe. In Android, changing a color in colors.xml updates every button and screen using that color, without touching a single line of Kotlin code.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A text-based format using nested tags to describe structured information, used for the Manifest and many resource files"?
choices: JSON
XML
APK
SDK

explanation.summary: XML (Extensible Markup Language) is a text-based way of describing structured information using nested tags, similar to HTML. Android uses XML extensively for files like the Manifest and many resource files.
lineByLine: "Text-based format" — an XML file is just readable text, not compiled binary code.
"Nested tags" — information is wrapped in labeled tags, like `<string name="app_name">Weather Now</string>`, and tags can contain other tags inside them.
"Describe structured information" — XML is good at representing hierarchical, organized data, like a Manifest listing components inside an application, itself inside the whole manifest.
AndroidManifest.xml, strings.xml, and colors.xml (among others) are all examples of files written in this format.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: XML is like a nested set of labeled boxes — a big box (the whole file) contains smaller labeled boxes inside it, which might contain even smaller labeled boxes. The AndroidManifest.xml file, for example, has a `<manifest>` box containing an `<application>` box, which itself contains `<activity>` boxes describing each screen.

question: What is "XML"?
choices: A text-based format using nested tags to describe structured information, used for the Manifest and many resource files
A collection of libraries, tools, and APIs for a specific platform
The final, installable package file that contains a compiled Android app
A stream of values over time that code can continuously observe

explanation.summary: XML is a text-based file format that uses nested, labeled tags to represent structured, hierarchical information. Android relies on XML heavily for files like the Manifest, strings, and colors.
lineByLine: XML tags look like `<tagname>content</tagname>`, and tags can be nested inside other tags to show structure.
The Manifest (AndroidManifest.xml) uses XML to nest components (like activities) inside the overall application declaration.
Resource files like strings.xml and colors.xml also use XML to define individual named values.
XML is human-readable text, which is part of why it's easy to review and edit these files directly.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: XML is like a nested set of labeled boxes, one inside another, showing how things relate and group together. In AndroidManifest.xml, a `<manifest>` box contains an `<application>` box, which contains one or more `<activity>` boxes — each level nested clearly inside the one before it.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A single screen in an Android app, hosting whatever UI is currently shown to the user"?
choices: Service
Activity
Context
Intent

explanation.summary: An Activity is Android's term for a single screen. It's the OS-managed component responsible for hosting whatever UI is currently displayed, and it's declared in the Manifest so the operating system knows it exists.
lineByLine: "Single screen" — one Activity typically represents one distinct screen the user sees, like a home screen or a settings screen.
"Hosting whatever UI is currently shown" — the Activity doesn't directly draw pixels itself; it hosts UI content (like Composables) via `setContent { }`.
Activities must be declared in the Manifest, or attempting to launch them will crash the app at runtime.
Only one Activity is ever the current, visible one, though others can exist "on hold" behind it in the back stack.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: an Activity is like a classroom you can walk into. It's a real, distinct place, and it's officially recognized (declared in the Manifest, like a room in the front-office binder). In Android, `class MainActivity : ComponentActivity()` is a typical Activity declaration, and `setContent { HomeScreen() }` is how it hosts the actual screen content shown to the user.

question: What is an "Activity" in Android?
choices: A component that can perform work in the background
A single screen in an Android app, hosting whatever UI is currently shown to the user
A component that listens for and reacts to system-wide announcements
An object that gives code access to app-wide and system-level resources

explanation.summary: An Activity represents a single screen in an Android app. It's an OS-managed component, declared in the Manifest, responsible for hosting whatever UI content is currently displayed to the user.
lineByLine: Activities are declared in the Manifest with an `<activity>` tag; an undeclared Activity will crash the app when the code tries to launch it.
An Activity hosts UI content (typically Composable functions) via a call like `setContent { }`.
Only one Activity is ever the current, visible screen at a time, though others may exist "on hold" in the back stack.
The Android operating system manages an Activity's lifecycle — creating it, pausing it, and eventually destroying it based on what the user is doing.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: an Activity is like a classroom you can walk into — a real, officially recognized place (declared in the Manifest). In code, `class MainActivity : ComponentActivity()` declares an Activity, and inside its `onCreate`, `setContent { HomeScreen() }` shows the actual screen content the user sees.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A component that can perform work in the background, independent of whether any screen is currently open"?
choices: Activity
Service
BroadcastReceiver
Context

explanation.summary: A Service is an Android component built specifically for doing work that doesn't need a visible screen, and — importantly — that can keep running even after the screen that started it closes.
lineByLine: "Perform work in the background" — a Service has no UI of its own; it just does a job, like syncing data or playing music.
"Independent of whether any screen is currently open" — unlike most app components, a Service's lifetime isn't tied to any specific Activity staying open.
A Service still needs to be declared in the Manifest, just like an Activity — being invisible doesn't exempt it from that requirement.
A Service that needs to keep running with the user's awareness (like ongoing music playback) is often promoted to a "foreground Service," showing a persistent notification.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Service is like a school custodian who keeps cleaning even after all the students have gone home for the day. Their job isn't tied to any specific classroom being occupied. In an app, a Service called `WeatherSyncService` might keep downloading forecast data in the background, even after the screen that started it has been closed.

question: What is a "Service" in Android?
choices: A single screen in an Android app
A component that can perform work in the background, independent of whether any screen is currently open
A request object used to start an Activity or Service
The set of states a component moves through over time

explanation.summary: A Service is an Android component designed to perform work in the background, with no screen of its own, and — critically — its running lifetime isn't tied to whether the screen that started it is still open.
lineByLine: A Service has no visible UI; its whole purpose is doing a job, like syncing data or downloading a file.
Even though it has no screen, a Service must still be declared in the Manifest, just like an Activity.
A Service's lifetime is independent of any specific Activity — closing the screen that started it doesn't automatically stop it.
A Service performing ongoing work the user should be aware of is often promoted to a "foreground Service," which requires showing a persistent notification.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Service is like a school custodian who keeps working even after students go home — their job isn't tied to any one classroom staying occupied. In an app, a Service can keep syncing data in the background, even after you've closed the screen that started it.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A component that listens for and reacts to system-wide or app-wide announcements, called broadcasts"?
choices: Service
BroadcastReceiver
Activity
Intent

explanation.summary: A BroadcastReceiver is the component built specifically to listen for and react to broadcasts — one-to-many announcements that many independent parts of a system can all notice and respond to at once.
lineByLine: "Listens for" — a BroadcastReceiver declares which specific kind of broadcast it cares about, like the device finishing booting up.
"System-wide or app-wide announcements" — broadcasts can come from the Android OS itself, or from an app's own code announcing something to itself.
"Broadcasts" — this specific term refers to the one-to-many messages that can be heard by many independent receivers simultaneously.
Unlike an Activity or Service, a BroadcastReceiver's reaction (onReceive) is meant to be brief — longer work should be handed off to a Service.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a BroadcastReceiver is like a classroom listening to the school's PA system. The principal's ONE announcement reaches every listening classroom at once, and each one independently decides how (or whether) to react. In Android, a BroadcastReceiver might listen for "the device just finished booting," reacting the same way any listening classroom reacts to a PA announcement.

question: What is a "BroadcastReceiver" in Android?
choices: A single screen in an Android app
A component that listens for and reacts to system-wide or app-wide announcements, called broadcasts
An object that gives code access to app-wide and system-level resources
A tool for scheduling work that should reliably happen eventually

explanation.summary: A BroadcastReceiver is an Android component that listens for and reacts to broadcasts — messages that can be heard by many independent parts of the system at once, rather than being addressed to just one specific destination.
lineByLine: A BroadcastReceiver declares which specific broadcast it cares about, like a network connectivity change.
Broadcasts can be sent by the Android operating system itself, or by an app's own code.
Because many receivers can listen for the same broadcast independently, this is fundamentally different from a private, one-to-one Intent request.
A BroadcastReceiver's onReceive function is meant to react briefly, often delegating any longer work to a Service.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a BroadcastReceiver is like a classroom listening to the school's PA system — ONE announcement reaches every listening room at once, and each decides independently how to react. In an app, a BroadcastReceiver might react to "the device just finished booting" the same way a classroom reacts to hearing a principal's announcement.

question: Which term matches this definition: "An object that gives code access to app-wide and system-level resources and capabilities, like starting other components"?
choices: Intent
Context
Resource
Package

explanation.summary: A Context is an object that grants code access to important app-wide and system-level capabilities — things like reading resources, starting other components, and accessing system services. Activities are one common example of something that is a Context.
lineByLine: "Gives code access to app-wide and system-level resources" — a Context can retrieve strings, colors, and other resources declared elsewhere.
"Starting other components" — you typically need a Context to launch a new Activity or Service.
An Activity IS-A Context (through inheritance), which is why Activities can freely do all these Context-related things.
Plain classes (not Activities) that need to do Context-dependent work usually need a Context explicitly passed in to them.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Context is like a staff ID badge that unlocks certain doors and privileges around a school building. An Activity naturally has this badge (since it IS a kind of Context), letting it access resources and start other components. A plain helper class without its own badge would need someone to lend it one (pass in a Context) before it could do the same things.

question: What is a "Context" in Android?
choices: A request object used to start an Activity or Service
An object that gives code access to app-wide and system-level resources and capabilities, like starting other components
A component that can perform work in the background
A stream of values over time that code can continuously observe

explanation.summary: A Context is an object that grants access to important app-wide and system-level capabilities, like reading resources, accessing system services, and starting other components such as Activities or Services.
lineByLine: Activities inherit from Context, which is why they can freely access resources and start other components.
Plain, ordinary classes that aren't Activities usually need a Context explicitly given to them if they need to do Context-related work.
Calling `getSystemService(...)` to retrieve a shared system tool (like NotificationManager) requires a Context.
Reading a resource, like a string or color declared in XML, also relies on having access to a Context.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Context is like a staff ID badge that unlocks certain doors and privileges in a building. An Activity has this badge built in (since it inherits from Context), letting it access resources and start other components directly. Other classes without their own badge need one lent to them (a Context passed in) before they can do the same things.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A request object used to start an Activity or Service, or to deliver a broadcast, either naming an exact target or describing a need"?
choices: Context
Intent
Package
Permission

explanation.summary: An Intent is a request object — a messenger — used to ask the Android system to do something, like open a specific screen, start a background helper, or send an announcement. It can either name an exact destination or describe a general need.
lineByLine: "Request object used to start an Activity or Service" — an Intent is how one component asks the system to launch another.
"Deliver a broadcast" — Intents are also the underlying mechanism used to send broadcasts to BroadcastReceivers.
"Naming an exact target" — an explicit Intent names one specific screen or Service by name, with no ambiguity.
"Describing a need" — an implicit Intent describes what should happen (like "share this text"), letting the operating system figure out who can help.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: an Intent is like a hall pass. A private, named hall pass (an explicit Intent) sends you to one exact classroom. A vague hall pass that just says "I need someone who can help with printing" (an implicit Intent) lets the front office figure out where to send you. In Android, `Intent(this, SettingsActivity::class.java)` is an explicit Intent naming an exact target screen.

question: What is an "Intent" in Android?
choices: A single path of execution that can run code
A request object used to start an Activity or Service, or to deliver a broadcast, either naming an exact target or describing a need
A collection of pre-written code you can add to a project
The environment where compiled code actually runs on a device

explanation.summary: An Intent is a request object used to ask the Android system to start something — an Activity, a Service, or to deliver a broadcast. It can either name an exact target directly (explicit) or describe what's needed and let the system figure out who can help (implicit).
lineByLine: An explicit Intent names a specific class directly, like `Intent(this, SettingsActivity::class.java)` — completely unambiguous.
An implicit Intent describes an action and some data, like "share this text," without naming a specific app or screen.
The Android OS resolves an implicit Intent by checking every installed app's declared intent-filters for a match.
Intents can also carry additional data alongside their addressing, called extras, so the target has information to work with once launched.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: an Intent is like a hall pass. A private, named hall pass (explicit Intent) sends you to exactly one classroom. A vague hall pass just describing a need (implicit Intent) lets the front office decide who can help. In code, `Intent(this, SettingsActivity::class.java)` names an exact destination, just like a private hall pass would.

question: Which term matches this definition: "An Intent wrapped up so that a different piece of code (like the notification system) can trigger it later, on the app's behalf"?
choices: Intent
PendingIntent
Context
Callback

explanation.summary: A PendingIntent is an Intent that's been wrapped and handed off to a different system (most commonly the notification system), so THAT system can trigger the Intent later, when something specific happens — like the user tapping a notification.
lineByLine: "An Intent wrapped up" — a PendingIntent doesn't replace a regular Intent, it packages one for delayed use.
"A different piece of code" — this is usually a system outside your own app's code, like the notification system.
"Trigger it later, on the app's behalf" — the notification system doesn't need to know what's inside; it just fires the wrapped Intent when the user taps.
This is exactly how tapping a notification opens a specific screen — the notification was holding a PendingIntent the whole time.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a PendingIntent is like handing a sealed envelope (containing a hall pass) to the school's front desk, saying "please deliver this exactly when the recipient checks in." The front desk doesn't need to know what the hall pass says — it just delivers it at the right moment. In an app, `PendingIntent.getActivity(context, 0, intent, FLAG_IMMUTABLE)` wraps up an ordinary Intent for a notification to fire later, when tapped.

question: What is a "PendingIntent"?
choices: A request object used to start an Activity or Service directly, right now
An Intent wrapped up so that a different piece of code (like the notification system) can trigger it later, on the app's behalf
A single screen in an Android app
A value that, when it changes, can automatically cause dependent UI to update

explanation.summary: A PendingIntent wraps an ordinary Intent, along with permission for a different system (typically the notification system) to fire that Intent later, at an appropriate moment, on the app's behalf.
lineByLine: A PendingIntent doesn't fire immediately — it's held onto and triggered later by whatever system it was handed to.
The most common use is attaching one to a notification, so tapping the notification fires the wrapped Intent.
The system holding the PendingIntent (like the notification system) doesn't need to understand what's inside it — it just triggers it at the right moment.
PendingIntent.getActivity(...) and PendingIntent.getBroadcast(...) are two common ways to create one, targeting either an Activity or a BroadcastReceiver.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a PendingIntent is like handing a sealed envelope to the school's front desk with instructions: "deliver this exactly when the recipient checks in." The desk doesn't need to know the envelope's contents. In an app, a notification holding a PendingIntent works the same way — tapping it triggers the wrapped Intent, opening a specific screen.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A traditional building block of Android UI, representing one on-screen element like a button or a piece of text"?
choices: View
Composable
Layout
Activity

explanation.summary: A View is the traditional (older-style) building block for Android user interfaces — one View typically represents one visible thing on screen, like a single button or a single line of text.
lineByLine: "Traditional building block" — Views were Android's original UI system, before Jetpack Compose existed.
"One on-screen element" — a Button is a View, a TextView (for displaying text) is a View, and so on.
Views are often arranged using XML layout files, describing how multiple Views fit together on one screen.
Modern Android development increasingly uses Composables instead of Views, though many existing apps still use Views extensively.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a View is like one specific piece of furniture in a classroom — a single desk, or the whiteboard. Multiple Views (pieces of furniture) are arranged together to build a complete classroom (screen). In older Android code, you'd see things like `<Button android:id="@+id/submitButton" />` inside an XML layout file, defining one specific View.

question: What is a "View" in Android?
