A traditional building block of Android UI, representing one on-screen element like a button or a piece of text
A single screen in an Android app
The arrangement describing how UI elements are positioned relative to each other

explanation.summary: A View is Android's original, traditional building block for UI — each View typically represents one visible element on screen, like a single button or a piece of displayed text.
lineByLine: Views predate Jetpack Compose and were the standard way to build Android UI for many years.
Common Views include Button, TextView (for text), and ImageView (for pictures).
Views are traditionally arranged using XML layout files that describe their positions and sizes.
Newer Android apps commonly use Composables instead of, or alongside, traditional Views.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a View is like one piece of furniture in a classroom — a single desk, or the whiteboard on the wall. Several Views combine to build a complete screen. In older-style Android code, you'd see something like `<TextView android:text="Hello" />` inside an XML layout, defining one specific View.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A function marked to describe a piece of UI in Jetpack Compose, Android's modern UI toolkit"?
choices: View
Composable
Layout
Resource

explanation.summary: A Composable is a Kotlin function, marked with a special @Composable annotation, that describes a piece of UI. Multiple Composables are combined together to build a complete screen, which is the foundation of Jetpack Compose, Android's modern approach to building UI.
lineByLine: "Function marked to describe a piece of UI" — the `@Composable` annotation is what lets a regular Kotlin function participate in describing UI.
"Jetpack Compose" — this is the name of Android's modern UI toolkit, which uses Composables instead of the older View system.
Composables can call other Composables, nesting smaller pieces of UI together to build a complete screen.
An Activity's `setContent { }` is where the hand-off from the Activity to a tree of Composables happens.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: if a View is like one specific piece of furniture, a Composable is more like a description of how a small section of a room should look, which can itself be built from smaller descriptions. A classroom's "reading corner" Composable might be built from a smaller "rug" Composable and a smaller "bookshelf" Composable. In code, `@Composable fun WeatherHeader() { Text("Today's Weather") }` is a simple Composable.

question: What is a "Composable" in Android?
choices: A traditional building block of Android UI, representing one on-screen element
A function marked to describe a piece of UI in Jetpack Compose, Android's modern UI toolkit
A single screen in an Android app
A file describing an app's structure and rules

explanation.summary: A Composable is a Kotlin function marked with @Composable, used to describe a piece of UI within Jetpack Compose, Android's modern UI toolkit. Composables are combined together, nested inside one another, to build up a complete screen.
lineByLine: The @Composable annotation is what allows a function to describe UI and call other Composables.
A larger screen is typically built from many smaller Composables, each responsible for one piece.
Composables can accept parameters, letting the same piece of UI be reused with different data.
An Activity's `setContent { }` call is the bridge point where Composables are actually displayed on screen.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Composable is like a description of a section of a room, which can itself be built from smaller sections. A "reading corner" section might be built from a "rug" section and a "bookshelf" section. In code, `@Composable fun WeatherHeader() { Text("Today's Weather") }` describes a small, reusable piece of UI.

question: Which term matches this definition: "The arrangement describing how UI elements are positioned relative to each other on a screen"?
choices: View
Composable
Layout
State

explanation.summary: A layout describes HOW multiple UI elements are positioned relative to one another — side by side, stacked vertically, or overlapping — rather than being a UI element itself.
lineByLine: "Arrangement" — a layout is about positioning and structure, not about the content itself.
"How UI elements are positioned relative to each other" — a layout decides whether things sit side-by-side, stacked, or overlapping.
In Compose, container Composables like Row, Column, and Box serve this exact arranging purpose.
In the older View system, XML layout files served this same purpose, describing how multiple Views should be positioned.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a layout is like the floor plan of a classroom, deciding where the desks, whiteboard, and reading corner each go relative to one another — separate from what those pieces of furniture actually look like. In Compose, `Row { Text("Monday"); Text("72°F") }` is a layout decision: it arranges the two Text elements side by side, horizontally.

question: What does "Layout" mean in the context of Android UI?
choices: A function marked to describe a piece of UI in Jetpack Compose
The arrangement describing how UI elements are positioned relative to each other on a screen
A single path of execution that can run code
A value that, when it changes, can automatically cause dependent UI to update

explanation.summary: Layout refers to the arrangement of UI elements — how they're positioned relative to one another, like side-by-side, stacked, or overlapping — as opposed to what those elements actually contain or look like individually.
lineByLine: Layout is about positioning and structure, distinct from the actual content being arranged.
In Jetpack Compose, container Composables like Row (horizontal), Column (vertical), and Box (overlapping) handle this arranging job.
In the older View system, XML layout files described this same kind of arrangement for Views.
The same content (like two pieces of text) can look very different depending purely on which layout arranges them.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a layout is like a classroom's floor plan — it decides where the desks, whiteboard, and reading corner go relative to each other, separate from what those pieces of furniture actually look like. In Compose, wrapping two Text elements in a `Row { }` arranges them side by side; wrapping the same two elements in a `Column { }` instead stacks them vertically.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A value that, when it changes, can automatically cause the UI depending on it to update"?
choices: Layout
State
Resource
Package

explanation.summary: State is a value Compose is specifically tracking — when that value changes, any UI depending on it can automatically update to reflect the new value, without you manually telling each piece of UI to refresh.
lineByLine: "A value" — state could be anything: a number, a piece of text, a true/false flag, or more complex data.
"When it changes" — the key part is that Compose is watching this value specifically for changes.
"Automatically cause the UI depending on it to update" — you don't manually refresh the screen; Compose does it for you, in a process called recomposition.
Tools like `remember` and `mutableStateOf` are how a piece of data gets marked as state that Compose should track this way.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: state is like a scoreboard at a game — the moment the score changes, everyone watching automatically sees the new number, without needing to be told to look again. In an app, a temperature value held as state means the on-screen Text automatically shows the updated number the instant that value changes, with no manual refresh code needed.

question: What does "State" mean in the context of Jetpack Compose?
choices: The arrangement describing how UI elements are positioned relative to each other
A value that, when it changes, can automatically cause the UI depending on it to update
A single screen in an Android app
A collection of pre-written code you can add to a project

explanation.summary: State refers to a value that Compose is specifically tracking, such that when the value changes, any UI depending on it can automatically update — without the developer manually refreshing anything.
lineByLine: State can hold any kind of value — numbers, text, booleans, or more complex data.
Tools like `remember` and `mutableStateOf` mark a value as state that Compose should watch for changes.
When state changes, Compose automatically re-runs (recomposes) just the parts of the UI that actually depend on it.
This automatic-update relationship is central to how Compose keeps the screen in sync with the app's underlying data.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: state is like a scoreboard at a game — the moment the score updates, everyone watching sees the new number automatically, without being told to check again. In Compose, a temperature held as state means the displayed number updates by itself the instant the underlying value changes.

question: Which term matches this definition: "The process where Compose automatically re-runs and redraws only the parts of the UI that depend on State that just changed"?
choices: Recomposition
Layout
Lifecycle
State

explanation.summary: Recomposition is the actual mechanism behind Compose's automatic UI updates. When a piece of State changes, Compose figures out exactly which Composables depend on it and re-runs (recomposes) just those pieces — not the entire screen.
lineByLine: "Automatically re-runs and redraws" — Compose handles this without you writing manual refresh code.
"Only the parts of the UI that depend on State that just changed" — recomposition is smart about scope; unrelated Composables aren't re-run unnecessarily.
This is the engine behind the earlier idea of State — State is the trigger, and recomposition is the actual response to that trigger.
Recomposition can happen very frequently and efficiently, since it typically only touches small, specific pieces of the UI.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: recomposition is like a scoreboard operator who only updates the specific numbers that changed, rather than repainting the entire scoreboard from scratch every time. In an app, if only a temperature value changes, Compose recomposes just the Text showing that number — not the whole screen around it.

question: What is "Recomposition" in Jetpack Compose?
choices: The set of states a component moves through over time
The process where Compose automatically re-runs and redraws only the parts of the UI that depend on State that just changed
A holder for UI-related data that survives configuration changes
Structured, organized storage for data that can be saved and queried later

explanation.summary: Recomposition is Compose's process of automatically re-running specific Composable functions when the State they depend on changes, updating just those parts of the UI rather than the entire screen.
lineByLine: Recomposition is triggered whenever a piece of tracked State actually changes value.
Only Composables that actually read that specific piece of State are re-run — unrelated parts of the screen are left alone.
This selective re-running is what makes Compose efficient, even with frequent small updates.
Recomposition is the direct mechanism that fulfills the promise behind State: 'when this value changes, dependent UI updates automatically.'
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: recomposition is like a scoreboard operator updating only the numbers that changed, rather than repainting the whole board from scratch. In an app, changing just a temperature value triggers recomposition of only the Text displaying that number, leaving the rest of the screen untouched.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A holder for UI-related data that survives configuration changes like screen rotation, keeping data separate from any one screen instance"?
choices: Activity
ViewModel
Database
Context

explanation.summary: A ViewModel is a special holder for UI-related data, deliberately kept separate from any one specific Activity instance, so it can survive events (like screen rotation) that would otherwise destroy and recreate the Activity holding that data.
lineByLine: "Holder for UI-related data" — a ViewModel typically holds things like a list of items to display, or the current state of a form.

"Survives configuration changes like screen rotation" — normally, rotating a phone destroys and recreates the Activity, wiping out any data it was holding directly. A ViewModel is specially retained across that exact event.
"Keeping data separate from any one screen instance" — this is exactly why the data survives: it was never actually owned by the destroyed Activity instance in the first place.
When a new Activity instance is created after rotation, it simply reconnects to the SAME ViewModel, recovering the data automatically.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a ViewModel is like a filing cabinet kept in the hallway outside a classroom, rather than inside it. If a fire drill causes the classroom itself to be completely reset (a fire drill = rotation), the filing cabinet in the hallway is untouched, and the class can pick right back up using the same files once they return. In an app, `WeatherViewModel` might hold the current forecast data, surviving rotation even though the Activity itself gets destroyed and recreated.

question: What is a "ViewModel" in Android?
choices: A single screen in an Android app
A holder for UI-related data that survives configuration changes like screen rotation, keeping data separate from any one screen instance
A message the operating system displays outside the app itself
A stream of values over time that code can continuously observe

explanation.summary: A ViewModel holds UI-related data separately from any one specific Activity instance, so that data can survive events like screen rotation, which would otherwise destroy the Activity and any data it was holding directly.
lineByLine: Rotation destroys the current Activity instance and creates a brand new one — but a ViewModel tied to that screen is deliberately kept alive by the OS through that exact event.
The new Activity instance, once created, simply reconnects to the SAME ViewModel, recovering whatever data it was holding.
This solves a real problem: without a ViewModel, information like a fetched list of items would be lost every time the phone rotates.
A ViewModel does NOT survive a full app close and reopening later — that requires genuine persistence tools instead, like a database.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a ViewModel is like a filing cabinet kept in the hallway rather than inside the classroom itself. If a fire drill (rotation) resets the classroom completely, the hallway filing cabinet is untouched, and class can continue using the same files once everyone returns. In an app, `WeatherViewModel` might hold forecast data that survives rotation, even though the Activity itself gets destroyed and recreated.

question: Which term matches this definition: "Structured, organized storage for data that can be saved, queried, and retrieved later, even after an app closes"?
choices: ViewModel
Database
Resource
State

explanation.summary: A database is a tool for genuinely saving structured data — like many records with several fields each — so it can be searched, sorted, and retrieved later, even long after the app has been completely closed and reopened.
lineByLine: "Structured, organized storage" — a database typically organizes data into tables, with defined fields for each record, like a forecast's city, date, and temperature.
"Can be saved, queried, and retrieved later" — unlike a ViewModel (which only survives rotation), a database genuinely persists data to disk.
"Even after an app closes" — this is the key distinction from temporary, in-memory data — a database survives a full app close and reopening, even weeks later.
In Android, Room is a common library used to work with a structured, on-device database.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a database is like a well-organized filing cabinet in a permanent records office, as opposed to a sticky note on your desk. The sticky note (in-memory data) is gone the moment you clean your desk; the filing cabinet (database) keeps everything safely organized, ready to look up again months later. In an app, hundreds of historical forecast records are exactly the kind of structured data suited to a database.

question: What is a "Database" in the context of an app?
choices: A holder for UI-related data that survives configuration changes
Structured, organized storage for data that can be saved, queried, and retrieved later, even after an app closes
A function marked to describe a piece of UI in Jetpack Compose
A running instance of an app on the device

explanation.summary: A database is a genuinely persistent storage tool for structured data — often many records, each with several related fields — that can be saved, searched, and retrieved even long after the app has been fully closed and reopened.
lineByLine: Data in a database is organized into tables, with each record having defined fields, like a forecast's city, date, and temperature.
A database is queryable — you can ask for specific subsets of data, like "all forecasts for Seattle," rather than only reading everything at once.
Unlike a ViewModel, which only survives configuration changes, a database survives a full app close, and even a device restart.
In Android, Room is a commonly used library that provides a structured, on-device database.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a database is like a well-organized filing cabinet in a permanent records office, unlike a sticky note that gets thrown away when your desk is cleaned. In an app, hundreds of historical weather records — needing to be searched and sorted later — are exactly the kind of data suited to a database rather than simple, temporary storage.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A stream of values over time that code can continuously observe, automatically reacting whenever a new value is emitted"?
choices: Database
Flow / StateFlow
Resource
Callback

explanation.summary: A Flow is a stream of values that can change over time, which other code can continuously watch. Rather than asking once "what's the current value?", observing a Flow means being automatically notified every time a new value comes through.
lineByLine: "Stream of values over time" — unlike a single one-time answer, a Flow can produce many values, one after another, as things change.
"Continuously observe" — code that's watching a Flow doesn't need to keep re-asking; it's notified automatically.
"Automatically reacting whenever a new value is emitted" — this is what makes a Flow useful for keeping a UI in sync with changing data, like a database table.
A StateFlow is a specific kind of Flow that always holds a current value, even before anything starts observing it.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Flow is like a subscription to a newsletter, rather than looking up a single fact once. Instead of checking a website repeatedly for updates, you subscribe once, and new issues (values) arrive automatically whenever there's something new. In an app, `dao.observeAllForecasts(): Flow<List<ForecastRecord>>` gives an ongoing stream that automatically emits a new list whenever the underlying database table changes.

question: What is a "Flow" (or "StateFlow")?
choices: Structured, organized storage for data that can be saved and queried later
A stream of values over time that code can continuously observe, automatically reacting whenever a new value is emitted
A single screen in an Android app
The set of states a component moves through over time

explanation.summary: A Flow represents an ongoing stream of values over time, rather than a single, one-time answer. Code observing a Flow is automatically notified whenever a new value comes through, without needing to repeatedly ask.
lineByLine: A Flow can emit many values over its lifetime, one after another, as the underlying data changes.
Observing a Flow means subscribing to it, rather than fetching a value just once.
This is commonly used to keep a UI automatically in sync with a changing database table — a new emission triggers a UI update.
A StateFlow is a specialized kind of Flow that always has a current value available, even before any observer starts watching it.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Flow is like subscribing to a newsletter rather than checking a website once for updates. New issues (values) arrive automatically whenever there's something new, without you needing to keep checking. In an app, a Flow from a database automatically emits a fresh list every time the underlying data changes, keeping the UI continuously up to date.

question: Which term matches this definition: "The set of states a component like an Activity moves through over time, controlled by the operating system rather than app code directly"?
choices: Lifecycle
Runtime
Process
State

explanation.summary: A Lifecycle is the sequence of states a component (most commonly an Activity) moves through — like being created, becoming visible, being paused, and eventually being destroyed. Crucially, the operating system decides when these transitions happen, not the app's own code.
lineByLine: "Set of states" — an Activity might be created, started, resumed, paused, stopped, or destroyed, among others.
"Moves through over time" — an Activity doesn't just have one fixed status; it transitions between states based on what's happening around it.
"Controlled by the operating system" — the app doesn't decide when to pause or stop; the OS calls specific functions (like onPause) at the appropriate moments.
Rotation is a dramatic example of a lifecycle event — it destroys the current Activity instance entirely and creates a brand new one.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Lifecycle is like a classroom's daily schedule — in session, at recess, locked up for the night — except the SCHOOL (not the classroom itself) decides when each transition happens. In an app, `onCreate`, `onPause`, and `onDestroy` are all specific Lifecycle callback functions the Android OS calls on an Activity at the appropriate moments.

question: What does "Lifecycle" mean in Android?
choices: A running instance of an app on the device
The set of states a component like an Activity moves through over time, controlled by the operating system rather than app code directly
A message the operating system displays outside the app itself
A tool for scheduling work that should reliably happen eventually

explanation.summary: Lifecycle refers to the sequence of states a component (typically an Activity) transitions through over time — created, visible, paused, stopped, destroyed — with the operating system, not the app's own code, deciding when each transition happens.
lineByLine: Common Lifecycle states include being created, becoming visible and interactive, losing focus, becoming fully hidden, and finally being destroyed.
The app doesn't call these transitions itself — the OS calls specific functions (like `onPause()` or `onStop()`) on the Activity at the right moments.
Rotation is one dramatic Lifecycle event: it fully destroys the current Activity instance and creates a brand new one.
Understanding an Activity's current Lifecycle state helps explain many behaviors, like why data can be lost on rotation unless it's specifically protected against that.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Lifecycle is like a classroom's daily schedule — in session, recess, locked up for the night — except the SCHOOL (not the classroom) decides when each transition happens. In Android, `onCreate`, `onPause`, and `onDestroy` are specific Lifecycle functions the OS calls on an Activity at appropriate times, not ones the app calls itself.

question: Which term matches this definition: "Formal approval an app must request before performing a sensitive action, like using the camera or finding a location"?
choices: Permission
Manifest
Context
Notification

explanation.summary: A Permission is formal approval an app must get before doing something sensitive — like accessing the camera, finding the user's location, or reading contacts. Simply declaring intent to do this in the Manifest isn't enough; for sensitive actions, the actual person using the phone must approve it too.
lineByLine: "Formal approval" — approval isn't automatic; it must be explicitly requested and granted.
"Before performing a sensitive action" — Permissions specifically apply to actions with real privacy or safety implications.
"Using the camera or finding a location" — these are classic examples of 'dangerous' permissions requiring explicit user approval, beyond just being declared in the Manifest.
Some Permissions (called 'normal') only need to be declared in the Manifest; others (called 'dangerous') additionally require the user's explicit approval at runtime.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Permission is like a signed permission slip required before a sensitive field trip — simply wanting to go isn't enough; a parent or guardian must actually approve it first. In an app, requesting the camera permission means the app must ask, and the actual user must tap "Allow," before the camera can be used.

question: What is a "Permission" in Android?
choices: A running instance of an app on the device
Formal approval an app must request before performing a sensitive action, like using the camera or finding a location
The ordered history of screens a user has visited
A message the operating system displays outside the app itself

explanation.summary: A Permission is formal approval an app must obtain before performing a sensitive action, such as using the camera, accessing location, or reading contacts. Some permissions only need a Manifest declaration; more sensitive ones also require the user's explicit approval while the app is running.
lineByLine: "Normal" permissions (like internet access) only require a simple Manifest declaration to be granted.
"Dangerous" permissions (like camera or location) require BOTH a Manifest declaration AND an explicit runtime approval from the actual phone user.
If a dangerous permission is denied, the app must handle that gracefully rather than assuming access will always be granted.
A user can revoke a previously granted permission at any time through the device's Settings, so apps should always check current status before relying on it.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Permission is like a signed permission slip needed for a sensitive field trip — simply wanting to go isn't enough; someone with actual authority (a parent) must approve it. In an app, requesting camera access means asking the user directly, and respecting whatever they decide.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A running instance of an app on the device, which the operating system can start, pause, or shut down as needed"?
choices: Runtime
Process
Thread
Lifecycle

explanation.summary: A Process is the actual running instance of your entire app on the device. The operating system can start it, and — importantly — can also kill it entirely if it needs the memory for something else, especially if your app isn't currently visible or active.
lineByLine: "Running instance of an app" — when you open an app, the OS starts a Process for it to run inside.
"The operating system can start, pause, or shut down as needed" — this is entirely the OS's decision, based on things like available memory.
If an app's screen is stopped and not visible, its entire Process becomes a candidate for the OS to kill, freeing up memory for whatever the user is currently using.
Information held only in memory (not genuinely saved/persisted) is lost completely if the Process is killed.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Process is like a single, complete performance of a play — if the theater needs the stage for something more urgent, it can end the current performance early, and if you want to watch again, a whole new performance has to start from the beginning. In an app, information only held in memory (not saved) is lost forever if the OS kills the Process running the app, exactly like a performance that gets cut short.

question: What is a "Process" in the context of an Android app?
choices: The environment where compiled code actually executes on a real device
A running instance of an app on the device, which the operating system can start, pause, or shut down as needed
A single path of execution that can run code
A tool that decides which thread a coroutine's work should run on

explanation.summary: A Process is the actual running instance of an app, managed entirely by the operating system. The OS can start it when the app is opened, and can also kill it entirely — especially if the app is in the background and the device needs memory for something else.
lineByLine: Opening an app starts a Process; the app's code actually executes within that Process.
The OS can kill a Process it decides isn't currently important, especially one whose screens are all stopped and invisible.
Information held only in memory (not genuinely saved to disk) is lost completely if the Process is killed.
Reopening the app after its Process was killed effectively starts a brand new Process from scratch.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Process is like one complete performance of a play — if the theater needs the stage urgently, the performance can be cut short, and watching it again means starting a whole new performance from the beginning. In an app, unsaved in-memory data is lost forever if the OS kills the Process, just like an interrupted performance can't simply resume where it left off.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A message the operating system displays outside the app itself, often used to inform the user about something happening in the background"?
choices: Intent
Notification
Permission
Callback

explanation.summary: A Notification is a message shown by the operating system, outside the app's own screen — appearing in the notification shade or as a banner — commonly used to tell the user about something happening in the background, or to prompt them back into the app.
lineByLine: "Message the operating system displays outside the app itself" — a Notification appears even if the app's own screen isn't currently open.
"Inform the user about something happening in the background" — this is especially common for Services doing ongoing work, like a download or sync.
Notifications must belong to a declared Notification Channel on modern Android before they can be shown at all.
Tapping a Notification commonly triggers a PendingIntent, opening a specific screen in the app.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Notification is like a note slipped under your door, letting you know something happened even while you weren't in the room. In an app, a foreground Service doing a long sync might show a Notification like "Syncing weather data... 45%" so the user knows work is happening, even without the app's screen open.

question: What is a "Notification" in Android?
choices: A message the operating system displays outside the app itself, often used to inform the user about something happening in the background
The ordered history of screens a user has visited
A component that can perform work in the background
A collection of libraries, tools, and APIs for a specific platform

explanation.summary: A Notification is a message displayed by the operating system, outside the app's own screen, commonly used to inform the user about background activity or to invite them back into the app.
lineByLine: Notifications appear in the notification shade or as banners, independent of whether the app's own screen is currently open.
A foreground Service performing ongoing work (like a sync) is required to show a persistent Notification while it runs.
On modern Android, Notifications must belong to a previously declared Notification Channel to be shown at all.
Tapping a Notification commonly fires a PendingIntent, opening a specific screen back in the app.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Notification is like a note slipped under your door, letting you know something happened while you weren't in the room. In an app, a background sync might show a Notification like "Syncing weather data... 45%," letting the user know without needing the app's screen open at all.

question: Which term matches this definition: "The ordered history of screens a user has visited, used to determine what appears when the user navigates backward"?
choices: Back Stack
Lifecycle
Layout
Process

explanation.summary: The Back Stack is Android's ordered record of every screen (Activity) the user has visited, in the order they visited them. It's what determines exactly what reappears when the user presses the back button.
lineByLine: "Ordered history of screens visited" — each new screen you open is stacked on top of the one that led to it.
"Used to determine what appears when the user navigates backward" — pressing back simply reveals whatever's next in that stack, not something arbitrary.
A screen isn't destroyed just because you navigated away from it — it's still "on hold" in the Back Stack, waiting to reappear.
This is sometimes represented as `[MainActivity, ForecastDetailActivity]`, with the last (rightmost) entry being the currently visible screen.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Back Stack is like a trail of breadcrumbs showing exactly which rooms you walked through to get where you are. Walking back simply retraces those same steps, one room at a time, in reverse. In an app, if MainActivity launches ForecastDetailActivity, the Back Stack becomes `[MainActivity, ForecastDetailActivity]`, and pressing back reveals MainActivity again, exactly where it was left.

question: What is the "Back Stack" in Android?
choices: A holder for UI-related data that survives configuration changes
The ordered history of screens a user has visited, used to determine what appears when the user navigates backward
A defined set of tools that lets two pieces of software talk to each other
A single path of execution that can run code

explanation.summary: The Back Stack is the ordered record of screens (Activities) a user has visited, with each new screen stacking on top of the previous one. It's exactly what determines what reappears when the user presses back.
lineByLine: Launching a new screen pushes it onto the top of the Back Stack, above whatever screen launched it.
The screen underneath isn't destroyed — it's simply not visible, patiently waiting its turn.
Pressing back pops the top screen off the stack, revealing whatever screen is now on top.
This ordered, stack-like structure is why backward navigation always feels predictable and consistent.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Back Stack is like a trail of breadcrumbs marking the rooms you walked through — retracing your steps means walking back through those same rooms in reverse order. In an app, if MainActivity launches ForecastDetailActivity, the Back Stack becomes `[MainActivity, ForecastDetailActivity]`; pressing back reveals MainActivity again, right where it was.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A single path of execution that can run code, with multiple threads allowing different work to happen in parallel"?

explanation.summary: A Thread is one single, ongoing path of execution capable of running code. A device can run multiple Threads at once, letting different pieces of work genuinely happen in parallel rather than strictly one at a time.
lineByLine: "Single path of execution" — a Thread runs instructions one after another, in order.
"Multiple threads allowing different work to happen in parallel" — a device isn't limited to just one Thread; several can run truly simultaneously.
An app has one particularly important Thread, often called the Main Thread or UI Thread, responsible for drawing the screen and responding to touches.
If a slow task runs directly on that one important Thread, the whole app can freeze, since that Thread can't do anything else while it's busy.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Thread is like one single worker doing tasks one at a time, in order. Having multiple Threads is like having multiple workers, each able to handle a different task at the same time. In an app, if a slow network call runs directly on the one Thread responsible for the screen, the whole app freezes — it's like the one worker in charge of answering the door being stuck doing something else entirely.

explanation.summary: The UI Thread (also called the Main Thread) is the ONE specific Thread responsible for drawing the screen and responding to taps and scrolls. If it gets blocked by something slow, the entire app appears frozen, since nothing else can happen on that same Thread in the meantime.
lineByLine: "The one specific thread" — out of potentially many Threads, this particular one has a special, singular responsibility.
"Responsible for updating the screen" — every visible change, animation, and touch response happens through this Thread.
"Must never be blocked by slow work" — if this Thread is busy with something slow (like a raw network call), the whole app becomes unresponsive.
Coroutines dispatched to a different Thread (like via Dispatchers.IO) are the standard way to keep slow work off this critical Thread.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: the UI Thread is like the one receptionist at a busy front desk — if they get stuck on a long phone call, nobody else can be helped, and the whole office appears "frozen" from a visitor's perspective. explanation.summary: The UI Thread (or Main Thread) is the single Thread in an app specifically responsible for drawing the screen and responding to user interaction. Blocking it with slow work causes the entire app to appear frozen and unresponsive.
lineByLine: Every screen update, animation, and tap response happens through this one particular Thread.
If a slow operation (like a direct, blocking network call) runs on this Thread, nothing else can happen until it finishes.
This is why slow work is typically moved to a different Thread, often via a coroutine dispatched with something like `Dispatchers.IO`.
A severely blocked UI Thread can eventually trigger an ANR ("Application Not Responding") warning from the OS.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: the UI Thread is like the one receptionist at a busy front desk — if they're stuck on a long call, nobody else gets helped, and the whole office seems frozen. In an app, a slow task running directly on the UI Thread has this exact effect on the entire visible screen.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A way to write code that can pause at slow operations without blocking the thread it's running on"?
choices: Thread
Coroutine
Dispatcher

explanation.summary: A Coroutine is Kotlin's tool for writing code that handles slow operations gracefully — it can pause at a slow point (like waiting on the network) without blocking the underlying Thread, which stays free to do other work in the meantime.
lineByLine: "A way to write code that can pause" — a coroutine can stop partway through, at a slow operation, and pick back up later.
"Without blocking the thread it's running on" — this is the key benefit: the Thread isn't stuck waiting; it's freed up for other work while the coroutine is paused.
Coroutines are started with functions like `launch { }` or `async { }`, typically inside a defined scope tied to something like an Activity's lifecycle.
A suspend function is the specific kind of function that can actually be paused this way, and it can only be called from inside a coroutine.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a coroutine is like starting a print job and walking away to do other things, instead of standing frozen at the printer the whole time. In an app, `lifecycleScope.launch { fetchWeatherFromNetwork() }` starts a coroutine that can pause during the slow network call, without freezing the rest of the app.

question: What is a "Coroutine" in Kotlin?
choices: A single path of execution that can run code
A way to write code that can pause at slow operations without blocking the thread it's running on
The final, installable package file that contains a compiled Android app

explanation.summary: A Coroutine lets code pause at slow operations, like a network call, without blocking the underlying Thread. While paused, that Thread is free to do other work, which is exactly what keeps an app's UI responsive during slow tasks.
lineByLine: Coroutines are typically started using functions like `launch { }` (fire-and-forget) or `async { }` (returns a result you can wait for).
A coroutine is usually tied to a specific scope, like `lifecycleScope`, so its lifetime is connected to something meaningful, like an Activity.
Inside a coroutine, suspend functions can be called — these are the specific functions capable of actually pausing without blocking.
Using coroutines correctly is the standard solution to the classic 'frozen app' problem caused by slow work running directly on the UI Thread.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a coroutine is like starting a print job and walking away to do other things, rather than standing frozen at the printer. In an app, `lifecycleScope.launch { fetchWeatherFromNetwork() }` starts a coroutine that can pause during a slow network call without freezing anything else.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A function marked so it can pause at a slow point without blocking, callable only from a coroutine or another suspend function"?
choices: Function
Suspend function
Callback
Dispatcher

explanation.summary: A suspend function is a specific kind of function marked with the `suspend` keyword, meaning it's allowed to pause partway through (typically at a slow operation) without blocking the Thread it's running on. It can only be called from inside a coroutine, or from another suspend function.
lineByLine: "A function marked so it can pause" — the `suspend` keyword is what signals this special pausing ability.
"At a slow point without blocking" — this is exactly the mechanism that lets code wait on something slow (like a network response) without freezing the underlying Thread.

"Callable only from a coroutine or another suspend function" — this restriction exists because pausability only makes sense within a context (a coroutine) that's actually capable of supporting a pause.
Trying to call a suspend function from a regular, non-suspend function results in a compile error, since there's no coroutine context available to support the pause.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a suspend function is like a recipe step that says "let it rest for 10 minutes" — the step itself is allowed to pause, but only within the context of actually following the whole recipe (the coroutine); you can't have a resting step floating around outside any recipe at all. In code, `suspend fun fetchWeatherFromNetwork(): Weather` can only be called from inside a coroutine, like one started with `launch { }`.

question: What is a "Suspend function" in Kotlin?
choices: A holder for UI-related data that survives configuration changes
A function marked so it can pause at a slow point without blocking, callable only from a coroutine or another suspend function

explanation.summary: A suspend function is marked with the `suspend` keyword, signaling that it can pause at a slow operation without blocking its underlying Thread. It's only callable from within a coroutine (or from another suspend function), since pausability requires that supporting context.
lineByLine: The `suspend` keyword marks a function as one that MIGHT pause partway through its execution.
This pausing happens without blocking the Thread — other work can continue while the suspend function is paused, waiting on something slow.
Attempting to call a suspend function from an ordinary, non-suspend function is a compile error, since there's no coroutine to support the pause.
This is exactly why you'll always see suspend functions called from inside something like `lifecycleScope.launch { }`.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a suspend function is like a recipe step that says "let it rest for 10 minutes" — the pause only makes sense within the context of following an actual recipe (a coroutine), not floating on its own. In code, `suspend fun fetchWeatherFromNetwork(): Weather` can only be called from inside a coroutine.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A tool that decides which thread or thread pool a coroutine's work should actually run on"?
choices: Coroutine
Dispatcher
Thread
WorkManager

explanation.summary: A Dispatcher decides WHERE a coroutine's work actually runs — which Thread or group of Threads (thread pool) it's assigned to. Different kinds of work (like UI updates versus network calls) fit different Dispatchers better.
lineByLine: "Decides which thread or thread pool a coroutine's work should run on" — this is the Dispatcher's whole job: choosing the right execution location for a given task.
`Dispatchers.Main` — runs work on the UI Thread, appropriate for anything touching the screen directly.
`Dispatchers.IO` — runs work on a Thread pool suited for slow, blocking-style tasks like network or disk access, kept off the UI Thread.
`Dispatchers.Default` — runs work on a Thread pool suited for heavy computation, like sorting large amounts of data.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Dispatcher is like an office manager deciding which specific desk or team should handle an incoming task — customer-facing requests go to the front desk (Main), heavy paperwork goes to the back office (IO), and complex calculations go to the analytics team (Default). In code, `lifecycleScope.launch(Dispatchers.IO) { ... }` explicitly tells the coroutine to run its slow work on the IO Dispatcher, not the Main one.

question: What is a "Dispatcher" in Kotlin coroutines?
choices: A function marked so it can pause at a slow point without blocking
A tool that decides which thread or thread pool a coroutine's work should actually run on
A single screen in an Android app
Structured, organized storage for data that can be saved and queried later

explanation.summary: A Dispatcher determines exactly which Thread or thread pool a coroutine's work actually executes on. Choosing the right Dispatcher matters, since different kinds of work — touching the UI, doing slow I/O, or heavy computation — each fit a different Dispatcher best.
lineByLine: `Dispatchers.Main` is for anything that needs to touch the UI directly, running on the UI Thread.
`Dispatchers.IO` is for slow, blocking-style work like network calls or database access, kept off the UI Thread.
`Dispatchers.Default` is for CPU-intensive computation, like sorting or processing large amounts of data.
`withContext(Dispatchers.Main) { }` is commonly used to briefly switch back to the UI Thread in the middle of otherwise IO-focused work, like updating the screen after a network call finishes.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: a Dispatcher is like an office manager assigning incoming tasks to the right team — customer requests to the front desk (Main), heavy paperwork to the back office (IO), complex calculations to the analytics team (Default). In code, `Dispatchers.IO` is explicitly chosen for slow network calls to keep them off the all-important Main Thread.

question: Which term matches this definition: "A tool for scheduling work that should reliably happen eventually, even if it needs to wait for certain conditions or survive the app closing"?
choices: Service
WorkManager
Coroutine
Dispatcher

explanation.summary: WorkManager is Android's tool for scheduling flexible, guaranteed-eventually background work. Unlike work that must happen immediately, WorkManager-scheduled tasks can wait for the right conditions (like network availability), and the request itself survives even if the app is closed or the process is killed in the meantime.
lineByLine: "Scheduling work that should reliably happen eventually" — WorkManager is for tasks where the exact timing isn't critical, just eventual completion.
"Even if it needs to wait for certain conditions" — WorkManager supports constraints, like requiring network access before running.
"Survive the app closing" — WorkManager persists its own scheduling information, so a request isn't lost even if the process is killed before it runs.
This makes WorkManager well suited for tasks like backing up photos or syncing data, where exact timing doesn't matter, but eventual completion does.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: WorkManager is like leaving a note for the maintenance crew: "please fix this whenever convenient, once the hallway is quiet — no rush." The note doesn't disappear if you go home for the day; the task will still get done eventually, once the right conditions line up. In an app, a WorkManager request with a network constraint patiently waits until the device actually has a connection, then runs — even if the app was closed the whole time.

question: What is "WorkManager" in Android?
choices: A component that can perform work in the background, independent of whether any screen is currently open
A tool for scheduling work that should reliably happen eventually, even if it needs to wait for certain conditions or survive the app closing

explanation.summary: WorkManager is Android's tool for scheduling background work that doesn't need to happen immediately, but should reliably happen eventually — even if it needs to wait for specific conditions, and even if the app's process is killed in the meantime.
lineByLine: WorkManager requests can include constraints, like requiring the device to have network access or be charging before running.
Unlike a Service (which is built for immediate, visible work), WorkManager is built for flexible-timing tasks, without needing a persistent notification.
WorkManager persists its own scheduling information to disk, so a request survives even a full process kill before it's had a chance to run.
Requests can also be chained together, so one task's completion triggers the next one in sequence.
explanation.tip: Real-world analogy: WorkManager is like leaving a note for the maintenance crew: "fix this whenever convenient, once the hallway is quiet." The note doesn't vanish if you go home for the day — the task still gets done eventually. In an app, a WorkManager request with a network constraint patiently waits for a connection before running, even surviving the app being fully closed in the meantime.
